food_name: Kuih Ko Swee (Steamed Rice Flour Jelly Kuih with Salted Grated Coconut)

local_names.romanized_local: Guǒ shuǐ (Mandarin); Ko Swee Kueh (Hokkien romanized)

chinese: 九层糕 (jiǔ céng gāo — similar but different item)

region_of_origin: Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia (Hokkien and Teochew Chinese communities)
food_subgroup: Steamed rice flour jelly kuih with fresh salted grated coconut topping; traditional Southeast Asian kuih category

preparation_method: served inverted with grated coconut topping

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of kuih ko swee is 4 pieces (approximately 120g), as commonly sold at Singapore and Malaysian wet markets and kopitiams.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo Database — Kuih Ko Swee / Ko Swee Kueh; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD steamed rice flour kuih entry
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for ko swee kueh / kuih ko swee (steamed rice flour jelly kuih with grated coconut), cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for steamed rice flour kuih. Values represent the composite dish including the salted grated coconut topping at the standard 100g:20g ratio. All per-100g values preserved from the verified input record (confidence 1.0 on all fields). Energy calculated as 4×protein + 4×carbohydrate + 9×fat: (1.8×4)+(33.0×4)+(2.5×9) = 7.2+132+22.5 = 161.7 ≈ 162 kcal — consistent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice flour-based steamed products; analogous estimate based on rice flour gel (GI ~72) from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on starch gelatinisation and retrogradation in steamed rice flour products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (RS3 resistant starch formation in cooled rice flour gels)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kuih ko swee exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed rice flour gel products. Rice flour has a published GI of approximately 72–78 (University of Sydney GI Database, rice flour entries). The gel structure formed during steaming and subsequent cooling promotes RS3 (retrograded resistant starch) formation, which moderates GI marginally compared to freshly cooked hot rice (GI ~72–89). GI 72 is the lower-bound defensible estimate for this cooled, steamed rice flour jelly product. GL per 4-piece serving (120g): (72 × 39.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 28.5 ≈ 29. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content — approximately 45.6mg per 4-piece serving (120g); sodium derives primarily from salted grated coconut topping rather than added salt in the kuih base
hypertension.guidance: Kuih ko swee is suitable for individuals with hypertension at a standard 4-piece serving, as the total sodium load (~45.6mg) is well below the 600mg per-meal threshold; however, patients should be aware that additional salted coconut topping increases sodium and should request lightly salted coconut where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load (GL 29 per 4-piece serving); the entire glycaemic load derives from rice starch with no dietary fat or protein in the kuih base to attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit kuih ko swee to 2 pieces per occasion (GL ~14, ~97 kcal) as an infrequent treat, and should pair it with a protein-containing food at the same meal to attenuate the postprandial glucose excursion driven by the high-GI rice flour base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (2.5g/100g) with saturated fat predominantly as lauric acid from coconut, which has a more neutral cardiovascular profile than long-chain saturated fatty acids; zero cholesterol and very low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kuih ko swee poses a low cardiovascular risk per serving due to its negligible cholesterol, low sodium, and low total fat content; the primary cardiovascular consideration is its high glycaemic load, which may contribute to postprandial triglyceride elevation if consumed in large quantities by individuals with dyslipidaemia.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Near-zero inflammatory load; coconut fat is predominantly lauric acid (less pro-inflammatory than palmitic acid); zero cholesterol and very low sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kuih ko swee is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the primary concern is caloric density in the context of weight management, as excess body weight increases joint load and systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Ko Swee (粿水, guǒ shuǐ) is a traditional steamed rice flour jelly kuih originating from the Hokkien and Teochew Chinese communities of Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia. It is made from a simple batter of rice flour, water, rock sugar, and pandan leaf extract, poured into small cup-shaped moulds and steamed until a semi-firm, translucent, jelly-like gel forms. The kuih is served inverted and topped with freshly grated coconut that has been lightly salted. The texture is soft, slightly chewy, and gelatinous, with a mild sweetness from rock sugar and a subtle pandan fragrance, complemented by the savoury, slightly oily freshness of the grated coconut topping. Kuih ko swee is a quintessential wet-market morning kuih and kopitiam snack in Singapore and Malaysia, typically sold alongside other traditional kuih at breakfast time. Nutritionally, a 4-piece serving (~120g) provides approximately 194 kcal, 2.2g protein, 3g fat (primarily from coconut), and 39.6g carbohydrate. Sodium is low at ~45.6mg per serving. The glycemic index is high (~72) and the glycemic load per serving is 29 (HIGH), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The food is free from eggs, dairy, wheat, and nuts, making it one of the broadest allergy-safe traditional kuih. It is vegan, halal, and gluten-free. The RS3 resistant starch formed during cooling of the rice flour gel moderates the GI marginally compared to freshly cooked hot rice, but the overall glycaemic impact remains high due to the large carbohydrate load per serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for steamed rice flour kuih; all per-100g fields carry verified confidence 1.0 in the input record. GI estimated at 0.9 confidence from University of Sydney GI Database analogous rice flour gel entries with RS3 retrogradation adjustment. Energy calculation cross-check confirms internal consistency (162 kcal). This is a well-documented traditional Singapore/Malaysian kuih with stable, simple ingredient composition.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for kuih ko swee (steamed rice flour jelly with grated coconut) is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of April 2026. The total fat (2.5g/100g) and saturated fat (2.2g/100g) are confirmed; the remaining ~0.3g/100g unsaturated fat is not fractionated in the available approved sources. 2. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for this specific steamed kuih preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo or MyFCD entries consulted. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — no hydrogenated fats in the ingredient list). 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived ingredients in the kuih base; coconut contains no cholesterol). 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Foodo for this food type; rice flour and coconut do not contribute meaningful amounts of these vitamins). 6. The input description incorrectly states 'wheat and tapioca flour and gula melaka' — this appears to be a copy-paste error from a different kuih record. Kuih ko swee is made from rice flour (not wheat or tapioca flour) and rock sugar (not gula melaka). The corrected ingredient list (rice flour, water, rock sugar, pandan, grated coconut) is used throughout this record. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) — no direct GI measurement for kuih ko swee exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 8. Tamil and Hindi local names are transliterations; no established native-script culinary term exists for this food in Tamil or Hindi culinary traditions, as it is a Hokkien/Teochew Chinese dish adopted into the Singapore multicultural food landscape.
